content:
**Linear regression** on continuous measurements predates modern ML. **Legendre** (1805) and **Gauss** (1809) formalized **least squares** — choosing parameters to minimize the sum of squared vertical errors — for astronomical orbit fitting.

**Gauss–Markov** reasoning (19th century) established that OLS gives the **best linear unbiased estimator** (BLUE) under classical assumptions.

**Galton** (1886) studied **linear regression** on hereditary heights — popularizing the term "regression" for the linear fit of y on x.

**Pearson** and early **biometrics** extended correlation and linear prediction across sciences.

The **normal equation** **w = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy** became standard once matrix notation unified multivariate OLS (20th century).

**Computers** shifted practice from hand inversion to **QR** and **SVD** decompositions for stability, and to **gradient methods** when **n** or **d** grew large.

Today **linear regression** remains the teaching default for **ŷ = Xw + b**, **MSE**, and bridges to **MLE** and **gradient descent** in the deep learning stack (linear output heads).

timeline.label: Least squares (1805–1809)
timeline.description: Legendre and Gauss minimize squared errors for astronomical data — origin of OLS.

timeline.label: Galton's regression (1886)
timeline.description: Linear fit of offspring on parent height; name "regression" enters statistics.

timeline.label: Gauss–Markov theorem (20th c.)
timeline.description: OLS as BLUE under homoscedastic, uncorrelated errors — theoretical foundation.

timeline.label: Matrix normal equation (20th c.)
timeline.description: w* = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy unifies multivariate linear regression in compact form.

timeline.label: Numerical LA + ML era (late 20th c.–present)
timeline.description: Stable lstsq/QR solvers and gradient descent at scale; linear heads in neural networks.
